title: The man who reads the water meters
summary: A reflective portrait of a meter reader whose unhurried, unnoticed rounds reveal a kind of attention the rest of the street has lost.
topic: What the patient, overlooked work of a meter reader reveals about attention and the dignity of unnoticed jobs
prompt: Write a 450–600-word reflective portrait of a single ordinary worker — a man who walks a city's streets reading water meters — observed closely from an outsider's viewpoint. Through concrete detail, reflect on the kind of attention his work demands and the dignity it holds despite going unnoticed. Avoid sentimentality; let detail carry the meaning. Concede one objection (that you may be romanticising him). Use cleft sentences, the passive voice, and varied sentence rhythm.
hintStarters: What I noticed first was not the meter but the way he…
Granted, it is easy for an onlooker to romanticise a job they would never…
It is attention, not strength, that a round like his actually…
We are taught to pity work like this, when what it might deserve is…

hintVocab.meaningVi: ghi lại một con số đọc được
công việc tẻ nhạt cực nhọc
chú tâm đến / để ý đến
dãi dầu / sờn cũ vì thời tiết
giữ nhịp độ riêng của mình
một bóng dáng quen thuộc của con phố
lướt qua mà không bị chú ý

sampleText:
He comes down our street in Montevideo on the second Tuesday of the month, always a little before nine, in a faded municipal jacket that fits no one well. I have watched him for a year now from a café window, and in that time not one person has greeted him by name. What I noticed first was not the meter but the way he kneels: slowly, one hand flat on the pavement, lowering himself to the box in the ground as though to read it he must come down to its level. He does not crouch and snatch a number. He settles.

The work is, on paper, nothing. He lifts a small iron cover, wipes the glass with his thumb, reads a figure, writes it in a ledger he still keeps by hand, and replaces the cover so that the slab sits flush. Then he stands, unhurried, and moves to the next house. A child could be taught the steps in a morning. And yet the steps are not the work. It is attention, not strength, that a round like his actually requires — the patience to give each of two hundred meters the same unrushed half-minute, the last as carefully as the first, on a street that will never know whether he hurried.

Watch him long enough and you see what the attention is made of. He notices things. A meter that has crept upward too fast — a leak no one has reported — earns a second look and a folded note left under a door. A house gone suddenly dry for a month gets a quiet word to a neighbour. None of this is in his job. It is simply what happens when a man moves through a street slowly enough to see it, in an age that has trained everyone else to move through it without looking up at all.

Granted, it is easy for an onlooker to romanticise a job they would never want and could leave at any time. I drink coffee while he kneels on cold stone; the patience I admire may, to him, be only the slowness of a body that aches, and the dignity I read into his rounds may be a story I tell to make my watching feel less idle. He has not asked to be a lesson. The risk of sentimentalising him is real, and I would rather name it than pretend I am above it.

Even so, naming the risk does not dissolve what I have actually seen. We are taught to pity work like this, when what it might deserve is a closer look at the kind of seeing it preserves. His job is one of the last that cannot be done quickly, that resists being turned into a swipe or a sensor, that still requires a person to stop, lower himself, and read. The city is quietly replacing men like him with devices that report from a distance. The numbers will be the same. The noticing will not.

I do not know his name. I am ashamed of that, after a year. But I know the shape of how he kneels, and I have come to think that a street is luckier than it understands to have, somewhere on its rounds, one person whose whole task is to pay slow attention to the things the rest of us have agreed to stop seeing.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: It-cleft to redefine what the job demands
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is attention, not strength, that a round like his actually requires' dùng câu chẻ it-cleft để chỉnh lại điều người đọc tưởng (sức lực) và nêu bật cái thực sự quan trọng (sự chú tâm).
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is attention, not strength, that a round like his actually requires' uses an it-cleft to correct the reader's assumption (strength) and foreground what the work truly demands (attention).

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to register the man's disappearance
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'We are taught to pity work like this', 'that resists being turned into a swipe or a sensor', và 'is quietly replacing men like him' dùng bị động để cho thấy cả thái độ thương hại lẫn sự thay thế ông đều là những điều áp đặt từ bên ngoài, ngoài tầm kiểm soát của ông.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'We are taught to pity work like this', 'that resists being turned into a swipe or a sensor', and the near-passive 'is quietly replacing men like him' show both the pity and the man's replacement as forces acting upon him from outside his control.

sampleTranslationVi:
Ông đi xuống con phố của chúng tôi ở Montevideo vào thứ Ba thứ hai của tháng, luôn hơi trước chín giờ một chút, trong một chiếc áo khoác công sở bạc màu chẳng vừa với ai cả. Tôi đã quan sát ông được một năm nay từ ô cửa sổ quán cà phê, và trong chừng ấy thời gian không một ai chào ông bằng tên. Cái tôi để ý đầu tiên không phải là cái đồng hồ mà là cách ông quỳ xuống: chậm rãi, một bàn tay áp phẳng lên mặt đường, hạ mình xuống cái hộp trong lòng đất như thể để đọc nó ông phải xuống ngang tầm với nó. Ông không cúi vội chộp lấy một con số. Ông an vị xuống.

Công việc, trên giấy tờ, chẳng là gì cả. Ông nhấc một cái nắp sắt nhỏ, lau lớp kính bằng ngón cái, đọc một con số, viết nó vào một cuốn sổ mà ông vẫn ghi tay, rồi đặt lại cái nắp sao cho phiến đá nằm bằng phẳng. Rồi ông đứng dậy, thong thả, và bước sang ngôi nhà kế tiếp. Một đứa trẻ có thể được dạy các bước trong một buổi sáng. Vậy mà các bước ấy không phải là công việc. Cái mà một tuyến đi như của ông thực sự đòi hỏi là sự chú tâm, chứ không phải sức lực — sự kiên nhẫn để dành cho mỗi cái trong hai trăm đồng hồ cùng một nửa phút không hối hả, cái cuối cẩn thận như cái đầu, trên một con phố sẽ chẳng bao giờ biết ông có vội vàng hay không.

Hãy quan sát ông đủ lâu và bạn sẽ thấy sự chú tâm ấy được làm bằng gì. Ông để ý mọi thứ. Một đồng hồ tăng lên quá nhanh — một chỗ rò chẳng ai báo — nhận được một cái nhìn lần thứ hai và một mảnh giấy gấp nhét dưới cánh cửa. Một ngôi nhà bỗng dưng khô cạn cả tháng nhận được một lời nhắn khẽ tới người hàng xóm. Không điều nào trong số này nằm trong công việc của ông. Đó đơn giản là điều xảy ra khi một người đi qua một con phố đủ chậm để nhìn thấy nó, trong một thời đại đã rèn cho mọi người khác đi qua nó mà chẳng hề ngước nhìn lên.

Công nhận, một kẻ đứng xem dễ dàng lãng mạn hóa một công việc mà họ sẽ chẳng bao giờ muốn làm và có thể bỏ đi bất cứ lúc nào. Tôi uống cà phê trong khi ông quỳ trên đá lạnh; cái kiên nhẫn mà tôi ngưỡng mộ có thể, với ông, chỉ là sự chậm chạp của một cơ thể đang đau nhức, và cái phẩm giá mà tôi gán cho những vòng đi của ông có thể là một câu chuyện tôi tự kể để việc mình ngồi xem bớt vô công rồi nghề. Ông đâu có xin trở thành một bài học. Nguy cơ ủy mị hóa ông là có thật, và tôi thà gọi tên nó ra còn hơn giả vờ rằng mình đứng trên nó.

Dẫu vậy, việc gọi tên nguy cơ ấy không làm tan biến những gì tôi thực sự đã thấy. Chúng ta được dạy để thương hại công việc kiểu này, trong khi cái nó có thể xứng đáng là một cái nhìn kỹ hơn vào kiểu quan sát mà nó gìn giữ. Công việc của ông là một trong những công việc cuối cùng không thể làm nhanh, thứ chống lại việc bị biến thành một cú quẹt hay một cảm biến, thứ vẫn đòi hỏi một con người dừng lại, hạ mình xuống, và đọc. Thành phố đang lặng lẽ thay thế những người như ông bằng những thiết bị báo cáo từ xa. Những con số sẽ vẫn vậy. Sự để ý thì không.

Tôi không biết tên ông. Tôi xấu hổ vì điều đó, sau một năm. Nhưng tôi biết dáng hình của cách ông quỳ xuống, và tôi đã đi đến chỗ nghĩ rằng một con phố may mắn hơn nó hiểu khi có, đâu đó trên tuyến đi của nó, một người mà toàn bộ nhiệm vụ là dành sự chú tâm chậm rãi cho những thứ mà phần còn lại chúng ta đã đồng ý ngừng nhìn thấy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to correct an assumption about what the job demands?
options: It is attention, not strength, that a round like his actually requires.
A round like his requires it is attention not strength actually that.
Strength not attention is what a round it requires like his actually.
Actually requires a round his like it is not strength attention that.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, not Y, that …' is an it-cleft foregrounding attention over strength.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is attention, not strength, that …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to present pity as something imposed from outside?
options: We choose freely to admire this work whenever we feel like it.
We are taught to pity work like this.
Everyone decides on their own to respect the meter reader.
Pity work like this we are who taught to.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'We are taught to pity' is passive, framing the pity as conditioning done to us.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'are + past participle' to show an imposed attitude?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence concedes the risk of romanticising most honestly?
options: Obviously I understand this man better than he understands himself.
Granted, it is easy for an onlooker to romanticise a job they would never want and could leave at any time.
Of course my admiration is always perfectly accurate.
Naturally, anyone watching from a café knows the truth of hard labour.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' honestly admits the writer's distance and the danger of idealising.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits the writer may be idealising from comfort?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'methodical' mean as it applies to the meter reader?
options: Careless and rushed
Done in a careful, orderly, step-by-step way
Loud and attention-seeking
Done by a machine
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Methodical' describes his orderly, unhurried, step-by-step rounds.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of working in a careful, systematic order.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from the concession back to the reflection?
options: In conclusion, I was completely wrong about him.
Even so, naming the risk does not dissolve what I have actually seen.
On the contrary, there is nothing worth noticing here.
Anyway, the man does not matter at all.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' accepts the risk of romanticising yet defends the genuine observation.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase grants the prior worry and re-centres what was seen?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent reflective register without slipping into sentimentality?
options: Honestly this poor old man just breaks my heart every single time, aw.
It is simply what happens when a man moves through a street slowly enough to see it.
Basically he's like the nicest, most amazing person ever, no joke.
It kind of makes me wanna cry watching him kneel, you know?
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B observes plainly and lets the detail carry meaning; the others gush sentimentally.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say the meter reader's job actually preserves?
options: A kind of slow seeing or attention
Physical strength and endurance
The city's water supply
His own private fortune
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues the work preserves 'the kind of seeing' — a patient attention.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread what the essay says deserves a 'closer look'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: He lifts a small iron cover, wipes the glass, reads a figure, and replaces the cover.
He lifts a cover, the glass wiping, to read a figure, and the cover replaced.
Lifting a cover, he wipes the glass, a figure read, and to replace the cover.
He a cover lifts, wipes glass the, figure reads a, replaced cover the.
mcQuestions.explanation: Four matched present-tense verb phrases form a clean parallel sequence of actions.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all the verbs in the same tense and form?

mcQuestions.prompt: What detail shows that the meter reader's attention goes beyond his job?
options: He wears a faded municipal jacket
He leaves a folded note about an unreported leak under a door
He arrives before nine each month
He writes figures in a ledger
mcQuestions.explanation: Noticing and reporting an unreported leak is care that 'is not in his job'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the act that is 'simply what happens' beyond his duties.

options: To describe something accurately and plainly
To make something seem more beautiful or noble than it really is
To pity someone deeply
To pay someone more money
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To romanticise' is to idealise something, which the writer admits he may be doing.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of making a hard reality seem prettier than it is.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which short sentence pair best uses rhythm to land the essay's point?
options: The numbers will be the same. The noticing will not.
The numbers and the noticing will both be the same as before, more or less.
While the numbers remain the same the noticing, it seems, will not however be.
The numbers will be, the noticing will not be, the same as they were before now.
mcQuestions.explanation: Two short, balanced sentences create a sharp contrast that carries the argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses two crisp, parallel sentences for emphasis?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's closing reflection?
options: The meter reader should be promoted immediately
A street is lucky to have one person whose task is to pay slow attention to what the rest have stopped seeing
Meter reading is the most important job in any city
Machines will always do the job better
mcQuestions.explanation: The final paragraph values the man for the slow attention he preserves on the street's behalf.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the essay's last sentence.

options: Highly skilled creative work
Dull, hard, monotonous labour
Well-paid office work
Work done by volunteers
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Drudgery' is tedious, wearisome work — what others assume the job to be.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of dull, grinding, repetitive labour.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best shows the essay resisting sentimentality by naming its own bias?
options: This man is a saint and everyone should worship him.
The dignity I read into his rounds may be a story I tell to make my watching feel less idle.
I am sure he loves every single moment of his beautiful work.
He is obviously the happiest worker in the entire city.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B turns the lens on the observer's own motives, the opposite of sentimentalising.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option doubts the writer's own admiration?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option lets concrete detail carry meaning rather than stating a moral?
options: He teaches us all the true value of patience and humility in life.
He does not crouch and snatch a number. He settles.
His job proves that ordinary people are the real heroes of society.
We must all learn to respect workers like this noble man.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B shows behaviour ('He settles') and lets the reader infer; the others moralise directly.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option describes an action instead of announcing a lesson?

options: Deeply caring and attentive
Showing no interest or concern
Highly skilled
Easily upset
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Indifferent' means lacking interest — the way the street treats the meter reader.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of not caring one way or the other.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a worker you pass regularly but have never really looked at. If you watched them for an hour, what might you notice that their job description leaves out — and why do you suppose you have not looked until now?
